subject: ap-world-history
title: Mongol Empire & Afro-Eurasian Trade
stimulus:
question on the lined page designated for that response. Each response is expected to fit within the space provided.
In your responses, be sure to address all parts of the questions you answer. Use complete sentences; an outline or
bulleted list alone is not acceptable. Y ou may plan your answers in this exam booklet, but no credit will be given for
notes written in this booklet.
Use the passage below to answer all parts of the question that follows.
“The Mongol conquests have been defined as the last chapter of the Eurasian transformations of the tenth
[through the] thirteenth centuries. Y et with the same, or even better, justification they can also be regarded as
the first chapter of a new era, perhaps the early-modern one. . . . T he Mongol period was a significant step
towards closer integration of the old world, both inside and outside the empire’s realm. Certainly the vast
dimensions of the empire contributed to that, but the role of the Mongols was not limited to [being] the
passive medium through which [their] subjects learned from one another. Instead they actively promoted
inter-cultural exchange.”
Michal Biran, historian, “The Mongol T ransformation: From the Steppe to
Eurasian Empire,” article published in 2004

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE economic development that would support Biran’s argument in the passage regarding the
Mongols and the “integration of the old world.”

scoring_points.description: Identify ONE economic development that would support Biran's argument regarding the Mongols and the 'integration of the old world.'

required_elements: Specific economic development linked to Mongol-era integration of Afro-Eurasia (e.g., expansion of Silk Road trade, growth of cities, safer long-distance travel for merchants).
alternatives.correct_example: The stability and size of the Mongol Empire led to the expansion of the Silk Road trade.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example from outside the Mongol period or unrelated to economic integration.
common_traps: Offering a political or cultural development instead of an economic one.
Naming the Silk Road without tying it to Mongol-era expansion or security.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE economic development that supports Biran's argument regarding the Mongols and the 'integration of the old world.' Examples that earn this point include: the stability and size of the Mongol Empire led to the expansion of the Silk Road trade; many cities in Afro-Eurasia grew larger and wealthier; merchants could travel vast distances across Afro-Eurasia easier and more safely under Mongol rule.

sample_responses.response: The stability and size of the Mongol Empire led to the expansion of the Silk Road trade.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — identifies a specific economic development (expansion of Silk Road trade) tied directly to Mongol rule and the integration theme.

sample_responses.response: Many cities in Afro-Eurasia grew larger and wealthier.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — urban growth is an economic development supporting the 'integration of the old world' argument.

sample_responses.response: Merchants could travel vast distances across Afro-Eurasia easier and more safely under Mongol rule.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — the Mongol-era security for long-distance merchants is a specific economic development that supports Biran.

parts.prompt:
Explain how ONE piece of evidence would challenge Biran’s argument regarding the Mongols and “the first
chapter of a new era.”

scoring_points.description: Explain how ONE piece of evidence would challenge Biran's argument regarding the Mongols and 'the first chapter of a new era.'

required_elements: Evidence and reasoning that pushes the start of the 'new era' out of the Mongol period or reframes Mongol rule as continuity/destruction rather than novelty.
alternatives.correct_example: The new era only began when connections between Afro-Eurasia and the Americas were established, not during the Mongol period.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Only restating Biran's argument without challenging it.
Citing Mongol-era integration without explaining why it does NOT mark a new era.
common_traps: Supporting Biran instead of challenging him.
Listing Mongol destruction without connecting it to the claim about the 'first chapter of a new era.'
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining how ONE piece of evidence would challenge Biran's argument regarding the Mongols and 'the first chapter of a new era.' Examples that earn this point include: the new era only began when connections between Afro-Eurasia and the Americas were established; the Mongol period only saw an expansion of previously existing trends in earlier decades and centuries; the Mongol conquests destroyed as much as they benefitted peoples of Afro-Eurasia.

sample_responses.response: The new era only began when connections between Afro-Eurasia and the Americas were established.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — pushes the start of the 'new era' to the post-1492 Columbian Exchange, challenging Biran's claim that the Mongol period was the 'first chapter.'

sample_responses.response: The Mongol period only saw an expansion of previously existing trends in earlier decades and centuries.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — reframes Mongol integration as continuity rather than a new era, directly challenging Biran.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquests destroyed as much as they benefitted peoples of Afro-Eurasia.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — emphasizes destruction (e.g., sack of Baghdad, demographic collapse) to undercut the claim that Mongol rule inaugurated a positive new era.

parts.prompt:
Explain how ONE intercultural transfer or exchange resulted from the Mongol conquests.
2
Use the map below to answer all parts of the question that follows.
PLAN OF “FACTORIES” (TRADING POSTS A ND W AREHOUSES) IN T HE
CITY OF CANTON, SOUTHERN CHINA, 1856
The plan was drawn from a survey of the area carried out by the British Royal Navy
Source: H. B. Morse, The International Relations of the Chinese Empire, (New Y ork: Paragon Book Gallery, 1910 ed.), p. 70.

scoring_points.description: Explain how ONE intercultural transfer or exchange resulted from the Mongol conquests.

required_elements: A specific intercultural transfer tied to the Mongol conquests with explanation of how it occurred (e.g., religious spread, technology transfer, exchange of knowledge).
alternatives.correct_example: Under the support of Mongol rulers, religions such as Islam or Buddhism spread.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Merely identifying a transfer without explaining its mechanism or Mongol connection.
Citing an exchange that pre-dates or post-dates the Mongol Empire.
common_traps: Naming a technology (gunpowder) without explaining how the Mongols facilitated its spread.
Discussing post-1450 exchanges that are not Mongol-era.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining how ONE intercultural transfer or exchange resulted from the Mongol conquests. Examples that earn this point include: under the support of Mongol rulers, religions such as Islam or Buddhism spread; Mongol conquests in Eurasia led to Mongol adoption of local religions and political practices; the Mongol unification of regions from China to the Middle East to Europe led to the spread of technologies, such as gunpowder, and pathogens, such as the plague; Mongol rule brought Islamic geographic, mathematical, and scientific knowledge to China.

sample_responses.response: Under the support of Mongol rulers, religions, such as Islam or Buddhism, spread.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — specific intercultural transfer (religion) directly connected to Mongol patronage.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol unification of regions from China to the Middle East to Europe led to the spread of technologies, such as gunpowder, and pathogens, such as the plague.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — names specific transfers (gunpowder, plague) and explains them as products of Mongol political unification.

sample_responses.response: Mongol rule brought Islamic geographic, mathematical, and scientific knowledge to China.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — specific directional knowledge transfer (Islamic world to China) directly enabled by Mongol rule.
